project_overview.project_name: Beirut Critical Environment Recovery, Restoration and Waste Management Program
project_overview.project_development_objective: To support immediate environment control measures from the impacts of August 2020 Port of Beirut explosion and planning for longer term environmental restoration efforts in Beirut City
project_overview.beneficiaries: Direct beneficiaries: 350,000 people (7.1% of Lebanese population) impacted by PoB explosion in Beirut. Indirect beneficiaries: 2,032,600 people in Mount Lebanon region

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Lebanon is classified as a fragile state facing severe economic crisis, political gridlock, weak governance, and hosting large refugee populations (851,717 registered Syrian refugees plus approximately 500,000 unregistered)

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Rehabilitation of damaged solid waste management infrastructure and management of asbestos-contaminated debris
key_components.description: Three sub-components: (1.1) Management of asbestos-contaminated CDW at Bakalian site and other locations, identification and preparation of disposal site, recycling of uncontaminated CDW (US$2.00M); (1.2) Rehabilitation of severely damaged Karantina sorting facility and Coral composting facility with technical support for sustainable operation (US$5.40M); (1.3) Piloting Integrated Solid Waste Management in explosion-affected areas with community engagement and women's participation (US$0.60M)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Policy and institutional support for greening Beirut's reconstruction agenda
key_components.description: Two sub-components: (2.1) Establishment of collaborative stakeholder platform for participatory planning of green reconstruction agenda with gender-balanced representation (US$0.30M); (2.2) Strengthening monitoring and enforcement for hazardous and chemical substances management, including inventory development, laboratory accreditation, and gender-differentiated exposure analysis (US$0.20M)

Project Management Unit staffing (project manager, environmental engineer, social and gender specialist, financial associate, procurement associate) and operational costs; Third-Party Monitoring Agency engagement for independent verification

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) as Intermediary Implementing Agency (IIA), with Project Management Unit based in Beirut office
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Coordination Committee (PCC) established within 3 months of effectiveness, including representatives from Ministry of Environment, Beirut Governorate, Municipality of Beirut, Council for Development and Reconstruction, and other stakeholders; meets quarterly and on-demand basis; Project Operations Manual (POM) prepared detailing implementation arrangements
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Alternative Procurement Arrangements (APA) - UNDP follows its own procurement procedures approved by World Bank; UNDP Procurement Services Unit provides oversight; three-level procurement oversight (CAP at business level >US$50,000; RACP at regional level US$500,000-US$2M; ACP at headquarters >US$2M); suppliers screened against World Bank Debarment and Sanctions Lists; bidders sign acceptance of World Bank Anti-Corruption Guidelines
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: UNDP responsible for results monitoring with M&E function assigned to establish baseline data and monitor progress; half-yearly monitoring of contaminated CDW management and waste management capacity indicators; Third-Party Monitoring Agency (TPMA) engaged by LFF for independent verification and quarterly reporting; quarterly Unaudited Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) submitted within 45 days of quarter end; annual audit included in UNDP corporate audit with public reporting; World Bank implementation support missions semi-annually

identified_risks.risk_description: Parliamentary elections scheduled for May 2022 may cause disruptions to government workflow; political gridlock preventing implementation of policy and reform agenda; citizen mistrust toward government escalated since October 2019 demonstrations and compounded by economic crisis and PoB explosion
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close monitoring of political developments and macroeconomic environment; implementation arrangement with UNDP as recipient to avoid delays and bottlenecks; World Bank engagement and coordination with implementing partners; strict screening and monitoring measures for transparency and accountability

identified_risks.risk_description: Lebanon facing most severe economic crisis in modern history; currency depreciation of Lebanese Pound against US dollar; hyperinflation (84.3% in 2020, 145% in 2021); GDP contraction of 21.4% in 2020 and 58.1% in 2021; shortage of US dollars; multiple exchange rates; capital controls; default on sovereign debt
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Direct fund transfers from World Bank to UNDP in fresh US$ to preserve purchasing power; regular review of benefit amounts in LBP; use of UNDP financial management system; quarterly financial reporting; TPMA verification of activities and payments

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak regulatory framework and technical capacity of institutions to manage hazardous and chemical substances; inadequate enforcement of existing regulations; absence of hazardous waste treatment and disposal facilities in Lebanon; limited capacity for collection and temporary storage only; weak environmental governance in Beirut
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component 2 supports strengthening policy and regulatory framework for hazardous waste and chemicals management; inventory of hazardous chemicals/substances; accreditation of environmental laboratories; close coordination with government and development partners; capacity building through 'learning by doing' approach with MoE technical team involvement

identified_risks.risk_description: Complex nature of asbestos materials management and solid waste management activities; asbestos-containing dust generation during handling of ACM waste affecting densely populated areas; safe handling of asbestos waste requires expertise not readily available in country; untested capacity of implementing agencies in ESF requirements
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Deployment of qualified and experienced international ACM management agency; careful design review; adoption of best available international practices; asbestos management procedures prepared and implemented by experienced contractor; occupational health and safety plan for handling special waste types; World Bank technical support and oversight

identified_risks.risk_description: Procurement risk rated high due to: deteriorating socio-economic situation affecting implementation; perception of corruption in fragile political environment; complexity of coordination with stakeholders; specialized contractors/consultants needed; high safeguards issues; low market response; potential COVID-19 related delays; currency crisis and devaluation; delays in fund flows due to economic crisis and bureaucratic procedures; limited government oversight on fund utilization
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Regular reporting on procurement process and contract implementation; close World Bank-UNDP coordination; hiring of qualified procurement staff; updated Procurement Plans and periodic progress reports; specialized qualification requirements in procurement; TPMA hired by LFF for verification and fiduciary assurance; advance planning and close coordination with UNDP approval committees; UNDP as main implementing agency with reliance on UNDP financial management system; direct fund transfers; detailed financial reporting; inclusion in UNDP yearly external audit

identified_risks.risk_description: High environmental risk from: asbestos-containing dust generation during ACM waste handling affecting neighboring densely populated areas; health and safety risks to workers handling ACM, broken glass, and other waste; improper disposal site management impacting soil and groundwater; inadequate waste management during facility restoration causing odors, insects/rodents, runoff; traffic risks during waste transport; air emissions and noise during restoration and transport; potential flora/fauna impacts and runoff to Beirut River and marine environment; cumulative impacts from other rubble removal activities emitting asbestos dust
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Asbestos management procedures by experienced contractor; waste management plan and occupational health and safety plan; proper site selection analysis with climate and disaster risk screening; measures to prevent contaminant migration, soil erosion, water resource protection; site stability and restrictions on future land use post-closure; tripartite agreement between landowner, MoE, and municipality on post-closure responsibilities; proper waste management plans for pilot projects; driving safety measures; precautionary measures for minimizing emissions; adjusted working hours; vegetation removal planning excluding high biodiversity areas; runoff management at Coral facility

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial social risk from: health and safety risks to predominantly unskilled labor force (50-60 laborers); citizen mistrust toward government; nuisances to residents from waste transport (noise, traffic); potential exclusion of vulnerable groups in stakeholder engagement; gender inequality and limited women's participation in waste management sector; differentiated exposure to contamination based on gender roles and biological differences
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Occupational health and safety plan; inclusive stakeholder engagement process with gender-balanced representation and quotas; communication and outreach plan with stakeholder mapping; grievance redress mechanism with confidentiality and anonymity principles; multiple uptake channels for vulnerable groups; referral pathways for SEA/SH complaints; women-led NGO/group outreach and technical support; gender-disaggregated data collection and analysis; targeted awareness campaigns; user feedback mechanisms; involvement of women in design, implementation, and evaluation of pilots; dedicated social and gender specialist in PMU

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial stakeholder risk from: range of stakeholders with varying interests; limited governance structures for environmental restoration agenda; potential conflicts between formal and informal waste management systems; sustainability challenges of pilot projects dependent on grant funding
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Pro-active and inclusive stakeholder and citizen engagement process; collaborative platform establishment with appropriate representation mechanisms; communication and outreach plan; close consultation with public and private stakeholders and civil society; coordination with development partners; PCC establishment for government ownership and participation; capacity building through knowledge transfer

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk from: upcoming parliamentary elections in May 2022 impacting coordination with local authorities; ongoing COVID-19 pandemic affecting implementation and mobilization of international experts; potential curfews and social distancing requirements; war in Ukraine potentially affecting supply chains and expert availability
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close monitoring of political and health developments; advance planning; flexible implementation arrangements; contingency planning for expert mobilization; coordination with development partners

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance redress mechanism established with principles of confidentiality and anonymity; multiple uptake channels to reach all stakeholders including vulnerable groups; timely closure of complaints; escalation to management as needed; trained and sensitized staff; referral pathways for sexual exploitation and abuse/sexual harassment (SEA/SH) complaints; World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel available for project-affected communities
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Substantial gender focus: women's labor force participation only 25% vs 76% for men; women hold 6 of 128 parliament seats and 5.4% of local government positions; gender roles restrict women's access to education, health, economic opportunities, resources, and decision-making; waste sector has low status for addressing gender issues; project will: reach out to women-led NGOs/groups; provide technical training and equipment support; ensure equal representation in collaborative platform with quotas; involve women in design and implementation of pilots; develop gender-disaggregated awareness campaigns; create user feedback mechanisms; analyze gender-differentiated exposure to contamination; support women's participation in recycling activities and formalization; dedicated social and gender specialist in PMU
vulnerable_groups: Syrian refugees (851,717 registered, ~500,000 unregistered) with 75% below poverty line and 50% below extreme poverty line
Low-income residents of Beirut affected by explosion
Informal waste-pickers and workers in waste sector
Women and women-led groups in waste management
Workers in waste management facilities (health and safety risks)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed with conflict sensitivity considerations: addresses immediate recovery needs from man-made disaster (PoB explosion); supports inclusive stakeholder engagement to rebuild trust in government; promotes transparency and accountability through grievance mechanisms and third-party monitoring; integrates gender and social inclusion to address structural inequalities; supports institutional capacity building for environmental governance; aligns with 'Build back better, greener, smarter' principles; recognizes citizen mistrust escalated by October 2019 demonstrations, economic crisis, and PoB explosion; includes mechanisms to challenge gender roles in waste management; supports participatory planning for green reconstruction agenda
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Lebanon classified as fragile state facing multiple overlapping crises: (1) Severe economic and financial crisis since October 2019 with currency collapse, hyperinflation (145% in 2021), sovereign debt default, capital controls, and 58.1% GDP contraction in 2021; (2) Political gridlock and weak governance preventing policy reform implementation; (3) Humanitarian crisis with 851,717 registered Syrian refugees plus ~500,000 unregistered (15.36% of population), with 75% below poverty line; (4) COVID-19 pandemic; (5) Man-made disaster (PoB explosion August 2020) killing 217, wounding 6,000, displacing 300,000; (6) Weak institutional capacity and regulatory framework; (7) Inadequate basic service delivery; (8) Structural gender inequalities; (9) Climate change vulnerability with rising temperatures, decreasing precipitation, sea level rise, and 85% urban coastal population concentration
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risks from: potential civil unrest and protests related to economic crisis and government mistrust; ongoing political instability with parliamentary elections May 2022; potential for implementation disruptions; need for secure waste management operations in densely populated areas; health and safety risks to workers and communities from hazardous waste handling; potential traffic and transportation security issues during waste transport; need for secure storage and disposal of hazardous materials to prevent accidents similar to PoB explosion
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Lebanon hosts large refugee population: 851,717 registered Syrian refugees (15.36% of all registered displaced Syrians) plus approximately 500,000 unregistered and displaced Syrians; according to UN-Habitat, 25% of refugees live in Beirut; socio-economic situation deteriorating with close to 75% below poverty line and over 50% below extreme poverty line; project benefits include improved waste management and environmental quality for refugee-hosting communities; project includes targeted engagement with vulnerable groups and marginalized communities in stakeholder platform; gender-differentiated needs of refugee and vulnerable populations considered in project design
